All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a distributed system for getting the slowest query from Google search

Design a distributed system that can identify and retrieve the slowest performing query from Google's search infrastructure, with capabilities for both historical analysis (previous day) and real-time monitoring.

Asked at:

Google

A query latency observability system is a distributed telemetry and analytics platform that collects per-request timing from a massive fleet of search servers and continuously identifies the slowest query over configurable time windows. Think of the live dashboards SREs use at Google or Bing to spot tail latency regressions and drill into the culprit request.

Interviewers ask this to see if you can reason about streaming data at hyperscale, compute accurate global aggregates (max/Top-1) across thousands of shards, and bridge real-time monitoring with batch, previous-day analysis. It tests your understanding of event-time windows, out-of-order data, hierarchical aggregation, sampling, and fault tolerance under heavy write throughput.

Community Solutions

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to view, in near real time, the slowest query and its latency across the entire search fleet for a chosen time window (e.g., last 1, 5, 15 minutes).

Users should be able to retrieve the slowest query for historical windows (e.g., previous day overall and by hour).

Users should be able to drill down into the slowest query’s metadata (e.g., anonymized query identifier, data center/region, service tier, experiment flag) to aid root-cause analysis.

Users should be able to configure filters and rules (e.g., exclude retries/bots, minimum occurrence counts, threshold-based alerts) to reduce noise and spurious outliers.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you compute the global slowest query in near real time across millions of requests per second without a single bottleneck?

How do you handle event-time correctness given clock skew and out-of-order or late-arriving telemetry?

How would you compute and serve the previous day’s slowest query efficiently and reliably?

How do you reduce noise and avoid false positives from one-off outliers while still catching true tail issues?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

The core requirement is to surface the current slowest query within seconds. A real-time updates pattern enables continuous ingestion, windowed computation, and pushing fresh results to dashboards and alerts without polling heavy backends.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Search fleets generate massive telemetry write volume. Designing append-only ingestion, partitioning keys (e.g., by time and region), and backpressure-aware pipelines is essential to avoid hotspots and data loss.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Producing previous-day results requires durable, batch-style computation over large log partitions, with retries, backfills, and fault tolerance—all hallmarks of long-running analytics tasks.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka is a de facto standard for high-throughput, durable telemetry ingestion with ordering guarantees per partition. It supports backpressure, replay for reprocessing, and geo-partitioning—ideal for collecting per-request latency events.

Read More
Flink

Flink provides event-time processing, watermarks, windowed aggregations, and exactly-once sinks. It’s well-suited to compute hierarchical global maxima in near real time and to run batch jobs for daily aggregates in a unified pipeline.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra offers high write throughput and time-series-friendly data modeling. It can store per-window aggregates and previous-day results with low-latency reads for dashboards, partitioned by date/region with TTLs for retention.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

YouTube Top K

Both require streaming computation of top results over time windows. While this problem focuses on Top-1 (max latency), the architectural patterns—partial aggregates, hierarchical reducers, and windowing—are the same.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Continuous ingestion of massive event streams with real-time and batch outputs mirrors ad click aggregation. Both demand event-time correctness, partitioning strategies, and cost-effective historical rollups.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Real-time visibility and rapid updates to users are central to both. Handling bursty, high-volume streams and delivering low-latency updates to a UI aligns with the live comments system’s challenges.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Centralizing aggregation on a single service or database node

This creates a scalability bottleneck and single point of failure. Under peak load, it will either throttle ingestion or produce stale/incorrect results due to dropped updates.

Using processing-time windows and ignoring clock skew/late events

This yields incorrect or flapping ‘slowest query’ results as delayed telemetry arrives. Event-time semantics with watermarks are necessary for correctness at scale.

Equating ‘slowest’ strictly with one-off max outliers without filters

A single pathological request can dominate, causing noisy alerts and wasted on-call time. You need frequency thresholds, error-class filters, or percentile corroboration to surface actionable issues.

Google

Staff

Search performance. We want the time between the user's Search query and the response to be minimal. Describe how you would implement an internal dashboard that will show us the slowest queries from the last day and help us debug the slowness.
Start by monitoring one server and scale up - more data, more servers, more QPS, etc..

In reality, Google gets the slowest/fastest search query the same exact way as they get the latency of any operation: using Monarch. You can stand out from all candidates if you are able to give a summery of how Monarch works.
